claims.claim_text: The opening bank migration story describes a large European bank with 400+ services, three tracing stacks, and disconnected payment traces.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: A platform team at a large European bank inherited a payment system with more than four hundred services and three separate tracing stacks. Legacy workloads emitted Zipkin spans, newer Java services sent data to Jaeger, and a Python rewrite had vendor-specific DataDog instrumentation baked into business code. When a card authorization crossed all three generations, the trace did not show a failed payment journey; it showed three disconnected fragments that each team interpreted differently.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large organizations often inherit multiple tracing systems across generations of services, so one end-to-end request can appear as disconnected fragments in different backends.
claims.rationale: This is a specific operational anecdote with named technologies and quantitative detail but no attributable real-world source.

claims.claim_text: The follow-on anecdote claims an audit required end-to-end regulated payment tracing and that the architect solved it with an OpenTelemetry migration layer.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: The problem became urgent when an audit required end-to-end transaction tracing for regulated payment flows. The expensive answer was to migrate every service to one commercial agent, rewrite old instrumentation, and accept a long period where observability would be worse before it became better. The platform architect chose a different route: put OpenTelemetry between the applications and the backends, let existing signals enter through compatible receivers, normalize the data in one Collector layer, and export to a single tracing backend while teams gradually cleaned up code.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In regulated environments, teams often need end-to-end tracing for critical transaction flows. A practical migration path is to place OpenTelemetry between applications and backends, ingest existing signals through compatible receivers, normalize them in a Collector, and migrate services incrementally instead of rewriting every application at once.
claims.rationale: This continues the same unsourced anecdote and should be rewritten as a general operational lesson without false specifics.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry lets application code emit telemetry without being tied to one observability backend.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. The Problem OpenTelemetry Solves, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The practical result is that your application code can describe what happened without caring whether the backend is Jaeger, Tempo, Prometheus, Loki, Honeycomb, Datadog, New Relic, or another system.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The practical result is that your application code can describe what happened without being tied to one observability backend.
claims.rationale: The backend-neutral lesson is well supported, but one source should not be forced to substantiate every named backend in the illustrative list.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry provides a common API and SDK, OTLP, and a Collector as the core vendor-neutral telemetry path.
claims.span_hint: section: 1. The Problem OpenTelemetry Solves, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The shared pool directly covers the Collector role and OTLP as the OpenTelemetry transport protocol.

claims.claim_text: The OpenTelemetry Java agent supports automatic instrumentation for Spring, JDBC, and common HTTP clients without application code changes.
claims.rationale: The official supported-libraries page directly lists Spring, JDBC, and multiple HTTP client instrumentations for the Java agent.

claims.claim_text: Python auto-instrumentation can instrument many common frameworks and client libraries without changing application code.
claims.anchor_text: A Python wrapper can instrument Flask, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, requests, and other common libraries.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A Python auto-instrumentation setup can instrument many common frameworks and client libraries without changing application code.
claims.rationale: The official Python zero-code docs support the broader auto-instrumentation capability, but not this exact enumerated library list in one place.

claims.claim_text: If `service.name` is not specified, OpenTelemetry SDKs fall back to an `unknown_service` value.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Instrumentation Strategy, paragraph after first Python trace example
claims.rationale: The semantic-conventions spec explicitly defines the `unknown_service` fallback for missing `service.name`.

claims.claim_text: Concrete paths such as `/orders/123` and `/orders/124` create separate Prometheus time series because label value changes create new series.
claims.span_hint: section: 2. Instrumentation Strategy, paragraph after metrics example
claims.rationale: Prometheus documents that time series are identified by metric name plus labels and that changing a label value creates a new series.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry also supports B3 propagation for compatibility with Zipkin-style environments.
claims.rationale: The Propagators API spec explicitly includes B3 among the propagators distributed by the OpenTelemetry project.

claims.claim_text: Collector pipelines are defined per signal and consist of receivers, processors, and exporters.
claims.rationale: The shared Collector source directly covers the receiver/processor/exporter pipeline model.

claims.claim_text: The default port for OTLP/gRPC is 4317 and the default port for OTLP/HTTP is 4318.
claims.span_hint: section: 6. Debugging an OpenTelemetry Pipeline, protocol mismatch paragraph
claims.rationale: The OTLP specification explicitly defines 4317 for OTLP/gRPC and 4318 for OTLP/HTTP.

claims.claim_text: Head sampling cannot guarantee that all error traces are kept, while tail sampling can keep traces based on completed-trace outcomes such as errors or latency.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Sampling, Filtering, and Cost Control, first two paragraphs
claims.rationale: The shared sampling source directly contrasts head and tail sampling and gives error and latency as tail-sampling examples.

claims.claim_text: Tail sampling in a multi-replica gateway requires trace-aware routing so all spans for a trace reach the same Collector instance.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Sampling, Filtering, and Cost Control, paragraph after tail-sampling thought exercise
claims.rationale: The official gateway deployment guidance explicitly discusses routing by trace ID so tail-sampling collectors see all spans for a trace.

claims.claim_text: A metric with unbounded identifiers can create millions of series.
claims.span_hint: section: 5. Sampling, Filtering, and Cost Control, final paragraph
claims.anchor_text: A metric with `user.id`, `order.id`, or full URL paths can create millions of series even when request volume is moderate.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A metric with `user.id`, `order.id`, or full URL paths can create very high-cardinality series even when request volume is moderate.
claims.rationale: The exact magnitude is illustrative rather than sourced here, so the operational lesson should be kept while removing the false precision.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry was formed by merging the OpenTracing and OpenCensus projects.
claims.rationale: The official OpenTelemetry overview page directly states that the project resulted from a merger of OpenTracing and OpenCensus.

claims.claim_text: Collector self-metrics such as exporter queue size and send failures help distinguish export problems from earlier pipeline problems.
claims.span_hint: section: 6. Debugging an OpenTelemetry Pipeline, paragraph 2
claims.rationale: The official internal-telemetry docs enumerate queue and send-failure metrics and explain how they indicate downstream exporter trouble.

claims.claim_text: The Collector can be deployed in multiple shapes such as agent, gateway, sidecar, or standalone process.
claims.rationale: The official deployment-pattern docs describe the supported Collector deployment shapes and when to use them.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Collector
further_reading.why_relevant: Best starting point for the receiver-processor-exporter model and why the Collector is the operational control point.

further_reading.title: OTLP Specification 1.10.0
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines OTLP transports, default ports, request/response behavior, and the protocol details that matter during debugging.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Traces
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers traces, spans, parent-child relationships, and the conceptual model behind propagation and trace debugging.

notes: Code blocks, quiz items, exercise steps, and tables were intentionally excluded unless their surrounding prose contained a load-bearing factual claim. The opening bank story appears composite and should be rewritten rather than cited.
